mapping.a_element: small visual perturbation applied to the WAM's input
mapping.b_element: small change in the rolling data window (overlapping windows sharing most of their data)

mapping.a_element: drift/misalignment between the imagined future and the executed action
mapping.b_element: projector movement D_{K,t} of the dominant eigenspace between windows

brief_justification: The only overlap is the word 'perturbation' and the loose notion that a small input change induces a measured drift; the driving machinery differs entirely. Paper A uses gradient-based adversarial optimization to craft input perturbations that desynchronize a neural world-action model, while Paper B uses random-matrix / Davis-Kahan first-order perturbation theory to derive null laws and calibrated hypothesis tests for covariance-estimator spectral functionals — no role-for-role mapping of a common governing process holds.
